Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: What is the current monetary threshold for the Small Claims Track in personal injury claims?
Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under CPR Part 26, the Small Claims Track for personal injury claims has a monetary limit of £1,500 (as of October 2023 reforms). A is incorrect as £1,000 is below the threshold. C (£2,000) and D (£5,000) are too high for personal injury small claims.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Crane & Pelletier is advising a client whose contract dispute has a value of £22,500. The client asks which procedural track will apply when proceedings are issued. There are no exceptional complexity factors and the claim is straightforward. To which track will the claim be allocated under CPR Part 26?

options.A: Small claims track — the claim is under £25,000 so falls within the small claims limit.
options.B: Multi-track — all contract claims are allocated to the multi-track regardless of value.
options.C: Fast track — the claim exceeds £10,000 but does not exceed £25,000 and is straightforward.
options.D: Intermediate track — claims between £15,000 and £50,000 must use the intermediate track.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Option C is correct. Under CPR r.26.9, the fast track applies to claims over £10,000 and not exceeding £25,000, where the trial is likely to last no more than one day and there are no complex expert issues. At £22,500 and straightforward, this claim falls squarely within the fast track parameters. Option A is wrong: the small claims track applies to most claims up to £10,000 (and up to £1,500 for personal injury and housing disrepair). £22,500 exceeds this threshold. Option B is wrong: contract claims are not automatically allocated to the multi-track; value and complexity are the governing criteria. Option D is wrong: the intermediate track (introduced October 2023) applies to claims over £25,000 and not exceeding £100,000 — this claim does not reach £25,000.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A document is sent by first class post at 10am on Tuesday. When is it deemed to be served under CPR r.6.14?

options.A: Tuesday (same day)
options.B: Wednesday (next business day)
options.C: Friday (third business day)
options.D: Thursday (second business day)

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.6.14 deems service by first class post to occur on the second business day after posting. If posted on Tuesday, the second business day after is Thursday. A is incorrect (same day service applies only to same-day delivery methods). B is the first business day only. C is too late.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Which of the following is NOT a formal requirement under CPR r.36.5 for a valid Part 36 offer?

options.A: It must be in writing
options.B: It must state the name of the claimant
options.C: It must be signed by the offering party's barrister
options.D: It must state that it is made under Part 36

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.36.5 does not require a Part 36 offer to be signed by a barrister; it can be made by a party or their legal representative. A is required (written form). B is required (identify the claimant). D is required (state it is made under Part 36). C misrepresents the rule—the offer need not be signed by counsel.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Pelletier is advising a client, Northgate Construction Ltd, who wishes to bring a claim against a contractor for breach of contract following defective building works. The client asks whether Qualified One-Way Costs Shifting (QOCS) will apply to protect them from an adverse costs order if the claim fails. To which of the following does QOCS NOT apply?

options.A: A personal injury claim arising from a road traffic accident.
options.B: A clinical negligence claim for injuries sustained during surgery.
options.C: A contract claim for breach of a building contract.
options.D: An employer's liability claim for injuries sustained at work.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Option C is correct. QOCS under CPR rr.44.13–44.16 applies only to claims for personal injury or death, and claims arising under the Fatal Accidents Act 1976. It does not apply to commercial or contract claims. Northgate's breach of contract claim against the contractor is not a personal injury claim and therefore falls outside QOCS. Option A is wrong: personal injury claims arising from road traffic accidents are a core application of QOCS. Option B is wrong: clinical negligence claims are expressly within the QOCS regime. Option D is wrong: employer's liability is a personal injury claim and QOCS applies.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Under CPR r.31.6, standard disclosure requires a party to disclose documents which are documents that the disclosing party has or has had in its power, except which of the following?

options.A: Documents relied upon in the case
options.B: Documents which adversely affect the disclosing party's case
options.C: Documents which support another party's case
options.D: Documents which may be harmful to an opponent and are deliberately withheld

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Standard disclosure under CPR r.31.6 requires disclosure of documents relied upon (A), adversely affecting one's own case (B), and supportive of any party's case (D). Deliberately withholding documents is misconduct, not a disclosed category. C is correct as it is not a permitted category.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Under CPR r.32.4, what is the maximum word limit for a witness statement in the Fast Track?

options.A: 500 words
options.B: 1,000 words
options.C: 2,000 words
options.D: Unlimited

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.32.4 provides that a witness statement in the Fast Track must not normally exceed 1,000 words. A (500 words) is too restrictive. C (2,000 words) applies to multi-track cases. D is incorrect as limits do apply.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Under CPR r.44.3(2), when the court awards costs on the standard basis, what must be proportionate?

options.A: The parties' incurred liability only
options.B: All costs incurred must be recoverable in their entirety
options.C: Costs which are proportionate to the complexity and value of the matter
options.D: Only costs agreed between the parties

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.44.3(2) requires that on the standard basis, costs must be proportionate to the complexity, importance and value of the matter. A is too narrow. B overstates recovery. D limits the rule to agreed costs only. C correctly states the proportionality requirement.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Under CPR r.52.3, on what test must a court give permission to appeal from a first instance decision?

options.A: The appeal has a real chance of success
options.B: The appeal is in the interests of justice
options.C: The appeal raises an important point of principle
options.D: The appeal raises a question of law or fact of general public importance

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.52.3 requires permission to appeal where the appeal has a real chance of success. B is too broad. C and D are tests for second appeals under CPR r.52.7. A correctly states the permission threshold.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Under the American Cyanamid test for interim injunctions, what is the first stage the applicant must establish?

options.A: That damages would be an adequate remedy
options.B: That there is a serious question to be tried
options.C: That the balance of convenience favours the granting of the injunction
options.D: That the status quo should be preserved

Dispute Resolution.explanation: American Cyanamid [1975] established three stages: (1) serious question to be tried, (2) adequacy of damages, and (3) balance of convenience. B is the first stage. A is the second stage. C is the third stage. D is a consequence, not a primary test stage.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant issues proceedings without serving the Pre-Action Protocol letter. How will the court typically respond under the overriding objective?

options.A: Dismiss the claim automatically for breach of protocol
options.B: Strike out the claim without any discretion
options.C: Consider the breach as a factor in case management and potentially award costs against the claimant
options.D: Require the claimant to start again with protocol compliance

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Protocol breaches are not automatic grounds for dismissal. The court has discretion and will consider the breach as a case management factor, potentially affecting costs under CPR r.44.2. A and B are too strict. D is not automatic. C reflects the flexible approach under the overriding objective.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant is domiciled in France and has no assets in England. The claimant wishes to sue in the English courts. What will be the primary legal barrier?

options.A: Lack of territorial jurisdiction under CPR r.6
options.B: The defendant is entitled to immunity as a foreign national
options.C: Only the French courts have jurisdiction over the defendant
options.D: The claimant must establish permission to serve out of the jurisdiction under CPR r.6.37

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under CPR r.6.37, a defendant domiciled abroad requires permission to serve out of the jurisdiction unless an exception applies. A misstates the rule. B is incorrect (no such immunity). C is too absolute. D correctly identifies the procedural requirement.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant has obtained default judgment against a defendant who failed to acknowledge service. The defendant wishes to appeal. What is the correct procedure?

options.A: The defendant must appeal as of right
options.B: The defendant must first seek to set aside the default judgment under CPR r.13.3
options.C: The defendant cannot challenge the judgment as default judgment is final
options.D: The defendant must file a substantive defence and proceed to trial

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.13.3 allows a defendant to apply to set aside a default judgment within specified time limits before an appeal. An appeal is not the first step. A is incorrect (no automatic right). C is too final. D bypasses the proper procedure. B is the correct route.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: The court issues an unless order requiring a party to serve a witness statement by 4pm on Friday. The statement is served at 4.30pm on Friday. What is the automatic consequence?

options.A: The witness statement is valid and the court will grant a brief extension
options.B: The witness statement is ineffective and the evidence cannot be used unless the party obtains relief from sanctions
options.C: The court must grant relief as a matter of course due to the minor breach
options.D: The breach is waived by the opposing party if they do not object immediately

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Unless orders are strict: failure to comply results in automatic consequences (here, invalidity) unless relief from sanctions is obtained under Denton. A suggests automatic flexibility. C reverses the test. D creates no automatic waiver. B correctly states that relief from sanctions is required.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party misses a disclosure deadline by 10 days due to administrative oversight. No prejudice results. Under Denton v TH White, what is the court likely to find at stage 1?

options.A: The breach is trivial and relief is automatically granted
options.B: The breach is not serious or significant and relief is likely
options.C: The breach is serious or significant because all deadline breaches are serious
options.D: The breach is serious unless the party can prove good reason

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Denton stage 1 assesses whether there was a serious/significant breach. A 10-day delay with no prejudice is likely not serious or significant. A is too absolute. C wrongly assumes all breaches are serious. D reverses the burden. B correctly reflects the proportionate approach.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A solicitor writes to a client advising on the legal implications of a contractual dispute. Three Rivers (No. 5) established that this communication is subject to legal advice privilege. Which of the following is NOT required for protection?

options.A: The communication must be made by a lawyer
options.B: The dominant purpose must be to provide legal advice
options.C: The communication must be made in anticipation of litigation
options.D: The client and lawyer must intend the communication to be confidential

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Legal advice privilege under Three Rivers protects communications between client and lawyer where the dominant purpose is legal advice, regardless of litigation. A is required (lawyer). B is required (dominant purpose). D is required (confidentiality). C is not required for legal advice privilege (only for litigation privilege).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A solicitor instructs a forensic accountant to prepare a report in anticipation of litigation, for use in legal advice. Is this communication protected by litigation privilege under Waugh v BRB?

options.A: No, because the accountant is not a lawyer
options.B: Yes, because it was created for the dominant purpose of litigation
options.C: No, because the accountant is a third party not engaged by the lawyer
options.D: Only if the client consents to the instruction

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Litigation privilege under Waugh protects communications with third parties (like accountants) where the dominant purpose is litigation. A wrongly restricts to lawyers only. C wrongly excludes third parties. D does not affect privilege. B correctly applies the wider scope of litigation privilege.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a Fast Track dispute involving a technical issue, the court has ordered a single joint expert. One party now wishes to adduce its own expert evidence. What must it establish under CPR r.35.7?

options.A: That the SJE is incompetent
options.B: That there is a genuine dispute between the parties on expert opinion
options.C: That the matter is outside the SJE's expertise and that court permission is required
options.D: That the SJE has been biased in favour of the other party

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.35.7 permits a party to instruct its own expert in addition to the SJE only with court permission and where appropriate. The party must obtain permission. A focuses on competence. B is too narrow. D requires proof of bias. C reflects the permission requirement correctly.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant seeks an interim injunction to restrain a competitor from using confidential information pending trial. The claimant has adequate funds to sue. Which American Cyanamid stage is likely to be most challenging?

options.A: Serious question to be tried—confidentiality claims are generally weak
options.B: Adequacy of damages—money damages cannot restore lost trade secrets
options.C: Balance of convenience—the competitor's business interests may outweigh the claimant's
options.D: The court's discretion to deny the injunction on general grounds

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Confidentiality breaches are difficult to remedy by damages alone, making damages inadequate. This typically makes the balance of convenience favour the claimant. A is incorrect (breach of confidence claims are normally strong). C may not be the hardest stage. D is not a stage of American Cyanamid. B correctly identifies the critical stage.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant seeks a freezing injunction to prevent the defendant from dissipating assets. What must the claimant establish as a prerequisite?

options.A: That the defendant has already begun disposing of assets
options.B: That there is a strong prima facie case on the merits and a real risk of dissipation
options.C: That the defendant's conduct has been dishonest or fraudulent
options.D: That any judgment will be for an amount exceeding £100,000

Dispute Resolution.explanation: A freezing injunction requires a strong prima facie case and a real risk that the defendant will dispose of assets. A is too narrow. C requires dishonesty but risk of dissipation suffices. D sets no financial threshold. B correctly states the prerequisites.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant made a Part 36 offer of £50,000. The claimant rejected it and obtained judgment for £48,000. What are the cost consequences under CPR r.36.17?

options.A: The claimant recovers costs as the offeree, regardless of the comparison
options.B: The defendant is liable for the claimant's costs up to the offer date, then the claimant pays the defendant's costs from that date
options.C: The claimant must pay the defendant's costs from the offer date onwards, plus the defendant's interest and a 10% uplift
options.D: There are no cost consequences as the claimant beat the offer in absolute terms

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.36.17 imposes indemnity costs, enhanced interest, and a 10% uplift when the offeree fails to beat a Part 36 offer. A ignores the consequences of not beating the offer. B reverses the flow. D is incorrect (consequences do apply). C correctly sets out the consequences.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant in a personal injury claim made a Part 36 offer of £60,000. The defendant rejected it and judgment was given for £58,000. What cost consequences follow?

options.A: QOCS protection is lifted and the claimant pays indemnity costs to the defendant
options.B: The defendant must pay the claimant's costs from the offer date with indemnity basis, interest, and enhanced uplift
options.C: QOCS protection remains but the defendant pays the claimant's costs on the standard basis
options.D: There are no cost consequences as QOCS protection is absolute in PI claims

Dispute Resolution.explanation: When the defendant fails to beat a claimant's Part 36 offer, even in a PI claim, the defendant pays the claimant's costs including indemnity basis and enhanced interest. A wrongly suggests QOCS is lifted. C understates the consequences. D wrongly treats QOCS as absolute. B correctly applies the consequences.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A creditor has an unsatisfied judgment against a defendant who owns a commercial property and has regular income from employment. What is the most appropriate enforcement method?

options.A: Writ of execution against the commercial property only
options.B: Combination of garnishee orders against employment income and execution against the property
options.C: Judgment debtor examination order to determine available assets, then selective enforcement
options.D: Attachment of earnings order only to avoid complexity

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.71 allows examination of the judgment debtor to establish what assets are available. This informs the best enforcement strategy. A is too limited. B may be premature without information. D ignores valuable property. C reflects best practice by investigating first.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party has systematically breached multiple unless orders, missing disclosure deadlines by weeks and fabricating evidence statements. Under Denton v TH White, what is most likely if stage 1 clearly establishes serious/significant breach?

options.A: Relief is automatically denied as the breach is serious
options.B: The court moves to stage 2 but the burden on the defaulting party is heavy to show good reason for relief
options.C: QOCS protection is invoked and relief is granted
options.D: The court must balance all CPR r.1.1 objectives before deciding stage 3

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Denton stage 1 (serious/significant breach) leads to stage 2 (good reason for breach and timeliness of application). Once a serious breach is found, the defaulting party bears the burden of showing good reason. A is too final. C does not apply. D is premature at stage 2. B correctly reflects the burden shift.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A PI claimant makes a Part 36 offer of £50,000 and later increases it to £70,000 (new offer). Judgment is given for £65,000. The defendant failed to beat either offer. How are costs and QOCS affected?

options.A: The claimant recovers costs on the standard basis; QOCS is not affected
options.B: The claimant recovers costs only from the second offer date with indemnity basis
options.C: QOCS is partially lifted and the claimant is liable for the defendant's costs from the offer date
options.D: The claimant recovers indemnity costs and interest uplift from the date of the first offer; QOCS remains intact

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.36.17 consequences apply from the first offer date if not beaten. QOCS does not prevent the consequences—instead, the claimant recovers enhanced costs while retaining QOCS protection. A understates consequences. C reverses the QOCS logic. D limits to second offer incorrectly. B is correct.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party sends a without prejudice settlement proposal to the opponent. The opponent later states in open court that the proposal was unreasonably low. Can the proposing party refer to the contents of the offer to defend its reasonableness?

options.A: No, without prejudice protection is absolute and cannot be breached even by the other party
options.B: Yes, the opponent has waived without prejudice protection by referring to it first
options.C: Only if the court grants permission in exceptional circumstances
options.D: No, unless the communications contain evidence of fraud or misconduct unrelated to settlement

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under Rush & Tompkins principles, if one party breaches without prejudice protection by referring to the contents, the other party may respond to defend itself. A is too absolute. C wrongly requires permission. D is too narrow. B reflects the waiver exception.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A multi-track case has had a costs budget approved by the court at £250,000. During trial, the party incurs costs of £300,000. At detailed assessment, the costs judge must determine what is recoverable. What is the interaction between the budget and this overspend?

options.A: The party can recover all reasonable costs of £300,000 as the budget is merely procedural
options.B: The approved budget acts as a ceiling, and costs above £250,000 are presumed unreasonable unless exceptional circumstances justify the overspend
options.C: The costs judge must award exactly £250,000 regardless of reasonableness
options.D: The budget is irrelevant at detailed assessment; the costs judge assesses independently

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.3.15 provides that costs budgets inform case management. An overspend creates a presumption of unreasonableness that the party must justify. A treats the budget as meaningless. C is too rigid. D wrongly divorces assessment from budgeting. B reflects the interaction correctly.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a contractual claim, the claimant applies for summary judgment. The parties genuinely dispute whether certain conduct amounted to a material breach of contract. The facts are not clearly one-sided. Under CPR r.24.2, what is the likely outcome?

options.A: Summary judgment must be granted as the claimant has a strong claim
options.B: Summary judgment must be refused as there is a genuine factual dispute
options.C: Summary judgment may be granted only if the claimant's argument is legally novel
options.D: The court must order trial to resolve the material breach dispute

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.24.2 requires that the respondent has 'no real prospect of success'. Genuine factual disputes typically prevent summary judgment—the matter requires trial. A is too presumptive. C is wrongly restrictive. D is not always required but is likely here. B correctly applies the test.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant obtained judgment in the county court for £30,000. The defendant appealed to the Court of Appeal. Now the defendant seeks a second appeal to the Supreme Court. Under CPR r.52.7, what must be established?

options.A: The second appeal has a real chance of success, as the first appeal test applies at all levels
options.B: That the appeal raises an important point of principle or practice, or some other compelling reason
options.C: That the Court of Appeal judgment contains a procedural error
options.D: That the value of the claim exceeds £100,000

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.52.7 applies a higher test for second appeals: important point of principle/practice or compelling reason. A wrongly applies the first appeal test. C is too narrow (errors of substance suffice). D sets no such threshold. B correctly states the second appeal test.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant (aged 16) received a Part 36 offer of £100,000 in a personal injury claim. The claimant's parent accepts on the child's behalf. Approval is sought under CPR r.21.4. If the Part 36 offer is rejected post-approval in favour of a higher judgment, what cost consequences follow?

options.A: CPR r.36.17 consequences do not apply because the claimant was an infant and parental consent is presumed reasonable
options.B: CPR r.36.17 consequences apply if the judgment exceeds the offer, but r.21.4 approval protects against sanctions
options.C: The normal Part 36 cost consequences apply; infant status and court approval do not shield from CPR r.36.17
options.D: Infant status voids the Part 36 offer and costs are awarded on the standard basis

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.36.17 applies regardless of infant status or parental/court approval of settlement. The offer is still binding if not beaten. A wrongly presumes immunity. B conflates approval with cost exemption. D wrongly voids the offer. C correctly applies Part 36 consequences.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant applies for an extension of time to serve particulars of claim. The defendant argues there is no good reason for the delay. Which principle does the court apply in deciding the application?

options.A: The court must grant any extension the parties agree to.
options.B: The court applies the overriding objective (CPR r.1.1) to deal with cases justly and at proportionate cost, including enforcing compliance with rules and orders, and has regard to the need for prompt and efficient resolution.
options.C: The court grants extensions automatically on a first application.
options.D: The court applies the slip rule to correct procedural errors.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.1.1 sets out the overriding objective: to deal with cases justly and at proportionate cost. This includes ensuring parties are on an equal footing, saving expense, dealing with cases in ways proportionate to their value and complexity, ensuring cases are dealt with expeditiously, and allotting an appropriate share of the court's resources. Under CPR r.1.2, the court must seek to give effect to the overriding objective when exercising powers under the CPR. Parties' agreement does not bind the court (A is wrong). Automatic extensions do not exist in this form (C is wrong). The slip rule (D) corrects accidental errors in judgments, not general procedural defaults.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Date of issue vs date of service
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant issues a claim form on the last day of the limitation period. The claim form is not served until 10 days later. Is the claim time-barred?

options.A: Yes — limitation runs until the date of service, not issue.
options.B: No — the claim is commenced, and the limitation period stopped running, on the date of issue (CPR r.7.2); service within the period allowed by CPR r.7.5 is sufficient.
options.C: Yes — the claim form must be issued and served on the same day.
options.D: No — but the defendant can apply to strike out for late service.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under CPR r.7.2, proceedings are commenced when the court issues a claim form. The limitation period is halted on the date of issue. CPR r.7.5 gives the claimant four months from the date of issue to serve the claim form within the jurisdiction (six months out of jurisdiction). The claim form is therefore not time-barred provided service occurs within the r.7.5 period. Option A confuses issue and service. Option C is wrong — same-day service is not required. Option D acknowledges the general position but inverts the conclusion: late service gives rise to possible sanctions or validity challenges, but on facts where service is within the r.7.5 period this does not arise.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant sues for breach of contract. Which of the following must the particulars of claim include?

options.A: A full summary of all evidence the claimant intends to rely on.
options.B: A concise statement of the facts on which the claimant relies, including the nature of the contract, the breach alleged, and the remedy sought (CPR r.16.4).
options.C: A list of all witnesses the claimant intends to call.
options.D: A costs estimate for the claim.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.16.4 requires particulars of claim to include: (i) a concise statement of the facts on which the claimant relies; (ii) if the claimant seeks interest, the basis for that claim; and (iii) if the claim includes personal injury, specified additional details. Particulars of claim are a statement of the claimant's case, not a summary of evidence (A is wrong). Witness lists are served separately as witness statements (C is wrong). A costs estimate appears in a costs budget (D is wrong).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claim for breach of contract is valued at £250,000. To which track will it most likely be allocated?

options.A: Small claims track — for claims up to £10,000.
options.B: Fast track — for claims up to £25,000.
options.C: Multi-track — for claims over £25,000 (or complex claims regardless of value).
options.D: Intermediate track — for claims between £25,000 and £100,000.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under CPR r.26.6, the multi-track is the normal track for any claim with a value exceeding £25,000 (or which cannot be justly dealt with on the fast track or intermediate track due to complexity). A £250,000 claim will be allocated to the multi-track. The small claims track (A) covers claims up to £10,000 (with lower thresholds for personal injury and housing disrepair). The fast track (B) covers £10,000–£25,000 claims that can be tried in a day. The intermediate track (D) was introduced by the Civil Procedure (Amendment No.2) Rules 2023 for straightforward claims between £25,000 and £100,000 — at £250,000 this claim goes to multi-track regardless.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a multi-track claim valued at £750,000, a directions questionnaire indicates the case will be complex. Which costs management obligation arises?

options.A: None — costs budgeting only applies to claims under £25,000.
options.B: Each party must file and exchange costs budgets (Precedent H) unless the court orders otherwise (CPR r.3.13); the court will approve or vary the budgets at a costs management conference.
options.C: Only the claimant must file a costs budget.
options.D: Costs budgets are filed only after judgment has been entered.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.3.13 requires each party (unless the court orders otherwise) to file and exchange costs budgets in the form of Precedent H. In multi-track cases where the claim is over £25,000 and costs management applies, budgets must be filed at least 21 days before the first case management conference. The court then undertakes costs management, approving or revising the budgets (CPR r.3.15). This helps control disproportionate costs. Option A is incorrect. Option C is wrong — all parties must file. Option D is wrong — budgets are filed before the first CMC, not after judgment.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant files a defence which amounts to a bare denial without any particulars. The claimant applies for summary judgment under CPR r.24. What must the claimant show?

options.A: That the defendant's case is weaker than the claimant's on the balance of evidence.
options.B: That the defendant has no real prospect of successfully defending the claim and there is no other compelling reason why the case should be disposed of at trial (CPR r.24.2).
options.C: That the claim is not disputed.
options.D: That the defendant has admitted liability.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.24.2 provides that the court may give summary judgment against a defendant on the whole or part of a claim if the court considers that the defendant has no real prospect of successfully defending the claim, and there is no other compelling reason why the case or issue should be disposed of at a trial. The 'real prospect' test requires more than a fanciful or theoretical prospect — there must be an arguable case with some degree of conviction. A bare denial may not satisfy this threshold. Option A introduces a comparative evidence test which is not the CPR r.24 standard. Options C and D are conditions not required by r.24.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant applies for an interim injunction to restrain the defendant from publishing allegedly defamatory material. The defendant argues the claimant has an adequate remedy in damages and the injunction should be refused. Which statement correctly applies the American Cyanamid test?

options.A: The court must conduct a mini-trial of the merits at the interim stage.
options.B: The court applies the American Cyanamid test: first, whether there is a serious question to be tried; second, whether damages would be an adequate remedy; and third (if not), whether the balance of convenience lies in favour of granting or refusing the injunction.
options.C: The claimant must prove the defendant's conduct is clearly unlawful before an injunction can be granted.
options.D: The defendant's argument is conclusive — if damages can compensate, an injunction must always be refused.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] established the modern test for interim injunctions: (1) Is there a serious question to be tried? (not a strong prima facie case); (2) Would damages be an adequate remedy for the claimant if the injunction is refused? If yes, the injunction will normally be refused. If no, (3) Would a cross-undertaking in damages adequately protect the defendant if the injunction is granted and the claimant later fails? If not, (4) The balance of convenience — where does the overall justice lie? The court does not conduct a mini-trial (A is wrong). Option C imposes too high a threshold at the interim stage. Option D overstates the adequacy of damages test — it is the first factor in the balance of convenience, not an absolute rule.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant applies without notice for a worldwide freezing order against the defendant, who is about to transfer assets abroad. What conditions must be satisfied?

options.A: A good arguable case on the merits; a real risk of dissipation of assets before judgment; and it must be just and convenient to grant the order.
options.B: The claimant must prove the defendant has already hidden assets.
options.C: The claimant must have obtained judgment before applying for a freezing order.
options.D: Only a final order can freeze assets — interim freezing orders are not permitted.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: A freezing injunction (Mareva injunction) requires: (i) a good arguable case on the merits of the underlying claim; (ii) a real risk of dissipation of assets or the defendant removing assets before judgment can be satisfied; and (iii) it is just and convenient to grant the order. A worldwide freezing order additionally requires assets or risk of dissipation with an international element. The applicant must give a cross-undertaking in damages. Option B imposes a higher threshold (proof of actual concealment) than is required. Option C is wrong — freezing orders are routinely granted before judgment. Option D is wrong — interim freezing orders are expressly available under Supreme Court Act 1981 s.37.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a multi-track claim, the court orders standard disclosure. What must a party disclose under CPR r.31.6?

options.A: Every document the party holds relating to the subject matter of the proceedings.
options.B: Documents on which the party relies; documents which adversely affect the party's own case; documents which adversely affect another party's case; and documents which support another party's case.
options.C: Only documents the party intends to rely on at trial.
options.D: Documents that are more likely than not to be relevant to the issues in dispute.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.31.6 defines standard disclosure as requiring a party to disclose: (a) documents on which it relies; (b) documents which adversely affect its own case; (c) documents which adversely affect another party's case; and (d) documents which support another party's case. This is limited to documents within the party's control. Option A describes a wider 'train of enquiry' disclosure that was the pre-CPR standard but is not standard disclosure. Option C is narrower than r.31.6. Option D introduces a likelihood test which is not the r.31.6 formulation.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A company receives an internal email from its in-house solicitor advising on the legal consequences of a proposed course of action. The opposing party seeks disclosure. Can the company claim legal advice privilege?

options.A: No — legal advice privilege only applies to communications with external solicitors.
options.B: Yes — legal advice privilege protects confidential communications between a lawyer (including in-house lawyers) and client made for the purpose of giving or receiving legal advice.
options.C: No — in-house lawyers' advice is subject to disclosure because their employment creates a conflict of interest.
options.D: Yes — but only if the in-house solicitor is also qualified as a barrister.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Legal advice privilege protects confidential communications between a lawyer and client that are made for the purpose of giving or obtaining legal advice. The privilege extends to in-house lawyers acting in their capacity as lawyers (Three Rivers DC v Bank of England (No.6) [2004]). The communication must be made in a legal capacity — commercial or management advice from an in-house lawyer is not protected. On the facts, the email concerns legal consequences, so it is properly characterised as legal advice. Option A incorrectly restricts privilege to external solicitors. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong — qualification as a barrister is not required.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Two parties are negotiating settlement. One party sends a letter headed 'Without Prejudice' offering £50,000 in full and final settlement. The offer is rejected. At trial, can the offering party's letter be disclosed to the court?

options.A: Yes — without prejudice correspondence must always be disclosed.
options.B: No — the without prejudice rule protects genuine settlement negotiations from being admitted in evidence (Rush & Tompkins v GLC [1989]); the offering party can prevent disclosure.
options.C: Yes — the without prejudice label is only a convention and does not provide legal protection.
options.D: No — but the receiving party can still disclose it if they wish.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: The without prejudice rule prevents evidence of settlement negotiations from being adduced in court without the consent of both parties. This applies where a document is made in the context of a genuine attempt to compromise a dispute and is marked (or implicitly) 'without prejudice' (Rush & Tompkins v GLC [1989]). Option A is wrong — the whole purpose of the rule is to prevent disclosure. Option C is wrong — the without prejudice label provides genuine legal protection in English law. Option D is wrong — the rule protects both parties; neither can waive it unilaterally (the privilege is joint).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Consequences of claimant beating its own offer
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a personal injury claim, the claimant makes a Part 36 offer of £100,000. The defendant does not accept. At trial, the claimant is awarded £120,000. What are the cost consequences?

options.A: Costs are awarded in the usual way — the winner pays nothing special.
options.B: Because the claimant has beaten its own Part 36 offer, the enhanced consequences under CPR r.36.17(4) apply: the claimant is entitled to indemnity costs from the date of expiry of the relevant period, enhanced interest on damages (up to 10% above base rate), enhanced interest on costs, and an additional amount (up to £75,000).
options.C: The defendant pays the claimant's costs on the standard basis only.
options.D: The Part 36 offer has no effect because the claimant won at trial anyway.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.36.17(4) provides that where a claimant obtains a judgment at least as advantageous as its own Part 36 offer, the court must (unless unjust) order: (i) indemnity costs from the date the relevant period for the offer expired; (ii) interest on those costs at up to 10% above base rate; (iii) interest on the award at up to 10% above base rate for the same period; and (iv) an additional amount (capped at £75,000) calculated as a percentage of the award/costs. These are significant financial incentives for defendants to accept reasonable offers. Option A misses the enhanced consequences. Option C underpays. Option D is wrong — Part 36 carries specific consequences even on a trial win.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Defendant's Part 36 offer — claimant fails to beat
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant makes a Part 36 offer of £80,000. The claimant rejects it and proceeds to trial, where the claimant is awarded £70,000. What costs consequences follow from CPR r.36.17(3)?

options.A: The claimant recovers all costs because the defendant lost.
options.B: Under CPR r.36.17(3), the claimant is entitled to costs up to the date the relevant period expired, but the defendant is entitled to costs from that date onwards; the court must so order unless it would be unjust.
options.C: The defendant bears all costs regardless of the outcome.
options.D: Each party pays their own costs.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.36.17(3) provides that where a claimant fails to obtain a judgment more advantageous than the defendant's Part 36 offer, the court must (unless unjust) order the claimant to pay the defendant's costs from the date the relevant period expired. This means: claimant recovers costs from issue to expiry of the relevant period; defendant recovers costs from expiry of the relevant period to judgment. The purpose is to incentivise acceptance of reasonable defendant offers. Option A ignores the Part 36 cost-shifting mechanism. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a fast-track claim for property damage valued at £15,000, both parties wish to appoint their own expert surveyors. What is the court most likely to order?

options.A: Each party may appoint their own expert without restriction.
options.B: The court will likely direct the appointment of a single joint expert (CPR r.35.7) to give evidence on the valuation issue, thereby saving cost and avoiding a battle of experts.
options.C: Expert evidence is not permitted on fast-track claims.
options.D: The court will appoint its own independent expert.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.35.7 empowers the court to direct that evidence on a particular issue is to be given by one expert only (a single joint expert), instructed by both parties. In fast-track cases, use of a single joint expert is strongly encouraged to control costs. The court will almost certainly refuse permission for each party to instruct separate experts for a £15,000 property damage claim. Option A ignores the court's case management powers. Option C is wrong — expert evidence can be used but is subject to court control. Option D describes court-appointed experts under r.35.15, which is rarely used in English civil litigation.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: An expert witness is instructed by the claimant in a multi-track claim. The expert's evidence, if given honestly, would substantially undermine the claimant's case. To whom does the expert's primary duty run?

options.A: To the instructing party who pays his fees.
options.B: To the court — an expert's overriding duty is to the court and not to the party who instructs or pays for the report (CPR r.35.3).
options.C: Equally to the court and the instructing party.
options.D: To the opposing party, to ensure a fair hearing.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.35.3 provides that it is the duty of experts to help the court on matters within their expertise. This duty overrides any obligation to the person from whom they have received instructions or by whom they are paid. An expert must give an honest opinion regardless of its impact on the instructing party's case. The expert should not act as an advocate. Options A, C, and D are all inconsistent with the duty under CPR r.35.3.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A costs order is made on the standard basis. Which statement correctly describes how costs are assessed on that basis?

options.A: All reasonable costs incurred, with any doubt resolved in favour of the paying party.
options.B: All costs incurred, without any proportionality test.
options.C: Costs are assessed on the basis of proportionality and reasonableness; any doubt about whether costs were reasonably incurred or are reasonable in amount is resolved in favour of the paying party (CPR r.44.3).
options.D: Costs are assessed at a fixed percentage of the damages recovered.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under CPR r.44.3, on the standard basis the court will only allow costs which are proportionate to the matters in issue and reasonable in amount. Critically, any doubt as to whether costs were reasonably or proportionately incurred is resolved in favour of the paying party. This differs from the indemnity basis (r.44.3(3)) where: (i) proportionality is not required; and (ii) doubts are resolved in favour of the receiving party. Option A describes indemnity basis costs. Options B and D are incorrect descriptions of either basis.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: CPR r.12 — entering and setting aside
Dispute Resolution.stem: The defendant fails to file an acknowledgment of service or a defence within the required time. The claimant enters default judgment. The defendant then applies to set it aside. Under CPR r.13.3, what must the defendant show?

options.A: That the judgment was entered without proper notice.
options.B: That the defendant has a real prospect of successfully defending the claim, or there is some other good reason to set aside the judgment or to allow the defendant to defend the claim.
options.C: That there was a procedural error by the court.
options.D: That the claimant consents to the judgment being set aside.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.13.3 provides that the court may set aside or vary a default judgment if the defendant has a real prospect of successfully defending the claim, or it appears to the court that there is some other good reason why the judgment should be set aside or varied, or the defendant should be allowed to defend the claim. The court must also consider whether the application was made promptly. This is a discretionary jurisdiction. Option A describes circumstances where there is a mandatory right to set aside (CPR r.13.2 — e.g., irregularly entered judgment). Option C is a narrower basis. Option D is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party wishes to appeal a circuit judge's decision on a point of law. What is the general test for permission to appeal?

options.A: The appeal has merit — the appeal court will re-hear the case.
options.B: The appeal has a real prospect of success or there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard (CPR r.52.6).
options.C: The party simply needs to file a notice of appeal within 21 days.
options.D: Permission is automatic for appeals from circuit judges.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.52.6 provides that permission to appeal may be given only where the court considers that the appeal would have a real prospect of success, or there is some other compelling reason for the appeal to be heard. Permission is required for most appeals (except certain mandatory categories). The appeal court does not generally re-hear the case (A is wrong) — it reviews the decision. Filing a notice of appeal (C) is a procedural step but does not grant permission. Permission is not automatic (D is wrong).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Two parties reach agreement at a mediation. They sign a written settlement agreement at the end of the session. The defendant later refuses to comply. How can the claimant enforce the settlement?

options.A: The claimant must issue fresh proceedings for breach of the settlement agreement.
options.B: The settlement agreement is a binding contract; if the terms have not been incorporated into a court order, the claimant may sue for breach of contract; if they have, the claimant may enforce by contempt proceedings or applying to execute the order.
options.C: Mediation agreements are not legally binding unless witnessed by a solicitor.
options.D: The claimant must return to mediation before taking legal action.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: A signed written settlement agreement reached at mediation is a binding contract in English law. Where the settlement has not been embodied in a court order (such as a Tomlin order), enforcement is by way of breach of contract proceedings. Where the terms have been incorporated into a court order, the claimant can enforce by contempt of court proceedings or by executing the order. A Tomlin order (CPR r.40.6) is commonly used to give court enforcement to settlement terms. Option A is partially correct but incomplete. Option C is wrong — there is no solicitor-witness requirement for a mediation agreement to be binding. Option D is wrong — return to mediation is not a prerequisite for legal enforcement.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: In a commercial dispute, the claimant wins at trial. It emerges that the claimant refused, without good reason, to engage with mediation proposed by the defendant. What costs consequences might follow?

options.A: None — the winner always recovers all costs.
options.B: The court may depart from the usual costs rule and reduce or deprive the winning claimant of some or all of its costs to reflect the unreasonable refusal to engage in ADR (Halsey v Milton Keynes NHS Trust [2004] / Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil [2023]).
options.C: The winning party receives costs on the indemnity basis to reflect the wasted trial.
options.D: The court cannot make any costs order against a winning party.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: In Halsey v Milton Keynes NHS Trust [2004], the Court of Appeal held that unreasonable refusal to engage in ADR can result in a costs penalty against the winning party. In Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2023], the Court of Appeal clarified that courts can compel (or strongly encourage) parties to engage in ADR, and that refusing without good reason can affect costs. The general rule is that the winner recovers costs, but this is subject to the court's discretion under CPR r.44.2 to take conduct into account. Option A is wrong — the winner is not automatically entitled to all costs. Options C and D are wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A solicitor sends a claim form to the defendant by first-class post on Monday. When is it deemed served under CPR r.6.14?

options.A: The second business day after posting — Wednesday (CPR r.6.14 — if posted on Monday, deemed served on Wednesday).
options.B: The day it is posted — Monday.
options.C: Seven days after posting.
options.D: When the defendant actually receives it.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.6.14 provides that a document served by first-class post (or other service where delivery is not the next business day) is deemed to have been served on the second business day after posting. If posted on Monday, the second business day after Monday is Wednesday (Tuesday being the first, Wednesday the second). This deemed service date applies even if the defendant receives the document earlier or later. Option B (day of posting) is wrong. Option C is wrong. Option D would require proof of actual receipt, which the CPR deemed service rule avoids.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: CPR r.22 — consequences of false statement
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party signs a statement of truth on its particulars of claim, knowing the facts stated are false. What are the potential consequences?

options.A: The party may be held in contempt of court (CPR r.32.14) if it is proved that there was no honest belief in the truth of the statement; the maker can be fined or imprisoned.
options.B: The court will automatically strike out the claim.
options.C: The case proceeds normally — statements of truth are merely administrative formalities.
options.D: Only the solicitor who signed is liable, not the party.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.32.14 provides that proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against a person who makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a document verified by a statement of truth, without honest belief in its truth. Contempt of court carries penalties including a fine and/or imprisonment. This applies whether the statement of truth is signed by the party or their legal representative. Automatic strike-out (A) is not the consequence — the court has discretion but contempt is the primary mechanism. Option C is wrong — statements of truth are a substantive requirement. Option D is wrong — the party who verifies the document is personally responsible.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant is sued by a claimant for negligence. The defendant believes a sub-contractor is partly responsible. The defendant wishes to seek contribution from the sub-contractor. What procedural mechanism is available?

options.A: The defendant may make a Part 20 claim (additional claim) against the sub-contractor within the existing proceedings, seeking contribution or indemnity without starting a separate action.
options.B: The defendant must issue separate proceedings against the sub-contractor.
options.C: The defendant must join the sub-contractor as a co-defendant by applying to the court.
options.D: The defendant must apply for a third-party notice at the start of proceedings only.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR Part 20 allows a defendant to make an additional claim (previously called a third-party notice) against any person not already a party to the proceedings, seeking contribution, indemnity, or any other remedy connected with the subject matter of the original claim. This avoids the expense of separate proceedings and keeps all related issues before the same court. A defendant may also bring a counterclaim against the claimant or make a claim against a co-defendant under Part 20. Option B would require separate proceedings unnecessarily. Options C and D describe incorrect procedures.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant has obtained a judgment for £85,000 against the defendant but the defendant does not pay voluntarily. Which of the following is a correct method of enforcing the judgment?

options.A: A writ of control (formerly execution against goods) allowing enforcement agents to seize and sell the defendant's goods.
options.B: Applying for the defendant's arrest.
options.C: Sending a statutory demand and issuing a winding-up petition immediately.
options.D: Reporting the defendant to the SRA.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: There are several methods of enforcing a money judgment: (i) writ of control / warrant of execution — enforcement agents seize and sell goods; (ii) charging order — over land or securities; (iii) third-party debt order (formerly garnishee) — attaches money owed by a third party to the defendant; (iv) attachment of earnings order; (v) insolvency proceedings (winding-up or bankruptcy). A writ of control is a standard and correct enforcement method. Arrest (A) is not a remedy for civil debt enforcement. A statutory demand leading to a winding-up petition (C) is a different route and requires meeting the insolvency threshold. Option D is irrelevant.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Before issuing a claim for professional negligence, a claimant's solicitor fails to follow the Pre-Action Protocol for Professional Negligence. What consequence may follow if the claim is eventually issued?

options.A: The court may impose costs sanctions, order a stay of proceedings to allow pre-action steps to be completed, or take the failure into account when making any costs order (PD Pre-Action Conduct and Protocols).
options.B: The court must strike out the claim.
options.C: There are no consequences — pre-action protocols are voluntary.
options.D: The claim is automatically time-barred.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Pre-Action Protocols and the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct require parties to follow the relevant protocol steps (letter of claim, response, disclosure) before issuing proceedings. If a party fails to comply, the court may: impose cost sanctions; order a stay to allow protocol steps to be completed; or otherwise take the non-compliance into account in its costs order. The protocols are not voluntary — non-compliance carries real consequences. However, the claim is not automatically struck out (A) or time-barred (D). Option C is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant who has been seriously injured applies for an interim payment before trial. Under CPR r.25.7, what must be established?

options.A: That the defendant has admitted liability, or the claimant would obtain judgment for a substantial sum if the matter proceeded to trial (unless the court considers it unreasonable), or the defendant is insured.
options.B: That judgment has already been entered on liability.
options.C: That the defendant has assets to satisfy any order.
options.D: That the application is made within 3 months of the claim being issued.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.25.7(1) provides that the court may make an order for an interim payment only if: (a) the defendant has admitted liability; (b) the claimant has obtained judgment against the defendant for damages to be assessed; or (c) the court is satisfied that, if the claim went to trial, the claimant would obtain judgment for a substantial amount of money against the defendant. The court must also take into account contributory negligence (r.25.7(5)). Option B is only one of three qualifying routes. Option C is not a requirement (though practical solvency is a factor). Option D is wrong — there is no fixed time limit for interim payment applications.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A solicitor is advising on where to issue a personal injury claim worth £60,000. Which court is most appropriate under the Civil Procedure Rules?

options.A: The County Court — personal injury claims below £50,000 must be issued in the County Court; between £50,000 and £100,000 can go to either the County Court or the High Court.
options.B: The Supreme Court.
options.C: The Magistrates' Court.
options.D: The High Court (Queen's Bench Division) only — claims over £15,000 must be issued in the High Court.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under the High Court and County Courts Jurisdiction Order 1991 (as amended), personal injury claims of less than £50,000 must be started in the County Court. For personal injury claims between £50,000 and below the financial threshold for mandatory High Court issue, claims may be issued in either court, though the County Court is generally preferred to manage resources. At £60,000, this claim may go to either the County Court or High Court, but the County Court is the more proportionate venue. Option B is wrong. Option C is wrong. Option D incorrectly states the threshold rules.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Solicitor's duty to the court — misleading the court
Dispute Resolution.stem: Ravi Sharma is a litigator at Crane & Pelletier. His client, Hassan Nazari, tells Sharma that a document Sharma is about to rely on in written submissions is actually a forgery Nazari created. Nazari refuses to allow Sharma to inform the court. What must Sharma do?

options.A: Sharma must withdraw from the case and (without revealing the reason to the other side) can no longer use the forged document; he must not mislead the court (SRA Code para 1.4 and 2.6).
options.B: Sharma must follow his client's instructions and file the document.
options.C: Sharma must report Nazari to the police immediately.
options.D: Sharma can file the document provided he adds a caveat noting it may be disputed.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: SRA Code para 1.4 prohibits a solicitor from misleading or attempting to mislead the court or others, either by their own acts or omissions or by being complicit in the acts of others. If a client reveals that evidence is forged and refuses to correct the position, the solicitor's duty to the court overrides the duty to the client. Sharma cannot continue to use the document and must withdraw from acting if the client refuses to allow disclosure. He must not inform the court of the reason for withdrawal (due to confidentiality) but must not misrepresent the situation either. Reporting to the police (C) is not required by the SRA Code and would breach confidentiality absent a specific exception. Option B requires Sharma to breach his duty to the court. Option D creates a misleading impression.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Duties in relation to opposing parties and solicitors
Dispute Resolution.stem: Diana Pelletier is acting for the defendant in litigation. She learns that opposing counsel has been sent, in error, a copy of a privileged document belonging to the defendant. She uses the document to prepare submissions before telling her client or the opposing solicitor. Which SRA Code obligation is engaged?

options.A: Pelletier should have notified the opposing solicitor immediately on recognising the document was confidential/privileged and returned or destroyed it without using it; using inadvertently disclosed privileged documents is a breach of professional duty and may constitute professional misconduct.
options.B: No obligation — the opposing party should have been more careful.
options.C: Pelletier can use any document received, regardless of how it was obtained.
options.D: Pelletier must use the document in the best interests of her client.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Where a solicitor receives a document that appears to be privileged by mistake, the solicitor should not read it further than is necessary to identify its nature, should notify the sender immediately, and should not make use of it. The SRA Principle 7 (acting in clients' best interests) and para 1.4 (duty not to mislead or take improper advantage) together require a solicitor not to exploit obvious mistakes by the opposing party. Using an inadvertently disclosed privileged document without notification amounts to professional misconduct and may lead to injunction proceedings requiring delivery up of the document (Pizzey v Ford Motor Co [1994]). Options A, C and D are all wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Gerald Crane, acting as a litigator, fails to file the trial bundle on time causing a trial to be adjourned. The court considers making a wasted costs order. Against whom can a wasted costs order be made and on what basis?

options.A: Against Crane personally (as the legal representative) — under s.51 Senior Courts Act 1981 and CPR r.46.8, a wasted costs order can be made against a legal representative whose improper, unreasonable or negligent act or omission causes another party to incur unnecessary costs.
options.B: Against the client — wasted costs orders are made against the losing party.
options.C: Against the firm — wasted costs orders cannot be made against individual solicitors.
options.D: Against the opposing party — wasted costs orders compensate the party who caused the wasted trial.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under Senior Courts Act 1981 s.51(6)-(7) and CPR r.46.8, the court may make a wasted costs order against a legal representative (barrister, solicitor, or other legal representative) where they have acted improperly, unreasonably or negligently, and their conduct caused a party to incur unnecessary costs. The order is made against the individual or firm of legal representatives, not against the client. The test requires: (i) improper/unreasonable/negligent conduct; (ii) the conduct caused the wasted costs; and (iii) it is just in all the circumstances. Option B is wrong. Option C is incorrect — individual solicitors can personally face wasted costs orders. Option D is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A homeowner discovers in January 2024 that a surveyor's negligent report from 2016 failed to identify structural defects. The six-year limitation period from the date of the cause of action expired in 2022. Can the homeowner still bring a claim?

options.A: Yes — the Limitation Act 1980 s.14A provides a three-year period running from the date of knowledge (when the claimant knew or ought reasonably to have known of the facts giving rise to the claim), subject to an overriding 15-year longstop from the date of the negligent act.
options.B: No — the six-year limitation period has expired and no claim can be brought.
options.C: Yes — but only if the surveyor is still solvent.
options.D: No — s.14A only applies to personal injury claims.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Limitation Act 1980 s.14A provides an extended limitation period for latent damage (other than personal injury) in negligence actions: a claimant may bring an action within three years of the date of knowledge (the date on which the claimant first had, or ought reasonably to have had, knowledge of the facts). If the homeowner first discovered the defects in January 2024, the three-year period runs until January 2027. However, s.14B imposes an absolute 15-year longstop from the date of the negligent act (here 2016), so the claim must be issued by 2031. Option B ignores s.14A. Option C introduces a solvency condition that does not exist. Option D is wrong — s.14A applies to all latent damage in negligence, not just personal injury (which has its own regime in s.11).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant repeatedly fails to comply with directions. The court makes an unless order: 'Unless the claimant files the witness statements by 4pm on 15 April 2024, the claim shall be struck out.' The claimant files at 5pm on 15 April. What happens?

options.A: The claim is automatically struck out on non-compliance with the unless order; the claimant must apply to reinstate and show good reason (CPR r.3.9).
options.B: The court gives the claimant additional time because the delay was only one hour.
options.C: The unless order is merely a warning; the court will not strike out without a further hearing.
options.D: The court converts the unless order to a peremptory order.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: An unless order operates automatically — if the specified condition is not met by the specified deadline, the sanction takes effect without further court order (CPR r.3.8). Filing at 5pm on a 4pm deadline means non-compliance, and the claim is struck out automatically. The claimant must then apply for relief from sanctions under CPR r.3.9, which requires the court to consider all the circumstances and in particular the need for efficient litigation and compliance with rules (following the guidance in Mitchell v News Group Newspapers [2014] and Denton v TH White [2014]). One hour's delay is not automatically forgiven — the Denton approach examines whether the breach is serious and significant, and if so, whether there is good reason. Option B is wrong. Options C and D are wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant's solicitor files a costs budget one day late due to a diary error. The opposing party objects and seeks to bar the defendant from relying on the budget. The court applies the Denton v TH White test. What three-stage analysis does the court apply?

options.A: Whether the breach was deliberate; whether the client was at fault; and whether the opponent is prejudiced.
options.B: Whether the breach causes injustice to the court; whether it is the first breach; and whether the solicitor has apologised.
options.C: (1) Identify and assess the seriousness and significance of the breach; (2) consider why the breach occurred; (3) evaluate all the circumstances of the case, including the need to conduct litigation efficiently and at proportionate cost, and enforce compliance with rules.
options.D: Whether the case has merits; whether the solicitor was negligent; and whether the opponent can recover costs.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: In Denton v TH White [2014] the Court of Appeal refined the approach to CPR r.3.9 relief from sanctions: (1) Was the breach serious or significant? If trivial, relief will usually be granted. (2) Why did the breach occur? Good reason may point to relief. (3) All the circumstances of the case — particularly the need for efficient, proportionate litigation and enforcement of rules. The Denton approach requires courts not to engage in a mechanistic or overly lenient approach. A one-day diary error may or may not be treated as trivial depending on the impact. Option A introduces irrelevant factors. Options C and D are wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Witness statements — exchange and use at trial
Dispute Resolution.stem: At trial, a party wishes to call a witness whose witness statement was not served and exchanged in accordance with the court's directions. What is the consequence under CPR r.32.10?

options.A: The witness may give oral evidence freely — witness statements are merely helpful.
options.B: The court must allow the evidence to avoid injustice.
options.C: Under CPR r.32.10, a witness may not give evidence in chief unless the court gives permission; failure to serve a witness statement in time means the party cannot call that witness to give evidence in chief without the court's leave.
options.D: The opposing party must consent before a late witness statement can be served.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.32.10 provides that if a witness statement is not served in accordance with the directions, the witness may not be called to give oral evidence in chief unless the court gives permission. The court's permission is required — not simply the opponent's consent. The court will exercise its discretion under the Denton/r.3.9 framework. Option A is wrong — witness statements are mandatory under the CPR regime. Option B is wrong — there is no automatic entitlement even to avoid injustice; the court has discretion. Option D is wrong — the court, not the opposing party, grants permission.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant brings a personal injury claim and loses at trial. The defendant applies for a costs order against the claimant. How does QOCS (qualified one-way costs shifting) affect this?

options.A: QOCS does not apply — the loser always pays costs in personal injury claims.
options.B: QOCS means the claimant can never be ordered to pay costs in any personal injury proceedings.
options.C: Under QOCS (CPR r.44.13–44.17), a claimant in a personal injury claim is generally protected from paying the defendant's costs if the claim is dismissed, subject to exceptions where the claim was found to be fundamentally dishonest or struck out.
options.D: QOCS applies only where the claimant has a conditional fee agreement.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: QOCS (CPR r.44.13–44.17) provides that orders for costs made against a claimant in personal injury proceedings will generally not be enforced without permission of the court, and permission will only be given where it would not be unjust to do so. This effectively protects losing personal injury claimants from paying the defendant's costs in most cases. However, QOCS protection is lost where the claim is found to be fundamentally dishonest (r.44.16), or where it is struck out for being an abuse of process or disclosing no reasonable grounds (r.44.15). Option A is wrong. Option B overstates the protection — the exceptions are important. Option D is wrong — QOCS replaced recoverability of ATE premiums and applies regardless of funding method.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Multiple claimants have claims arising from the same alleged defective product. Their solicitor applies for a Group Litigation Order (GLO). What is the purpose of a GLO?

options.A: To merge all individual claims into one single claim.
options.B: To remove the need for individual claimants to prove their own losses.
options.C: To provide for the case management of claims which give rise to common or related issues of fact or law (CPR r.19.22), allowing the court to manage multiple related claims efficiently.
options.D: To cap the total damages recoverable across all claims.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.19.22 provides for a Group Litigation Order to provide for the case management of claims which give rise to common or related issues of fact or law. Once a GLO is made, a group register is maintained and the court may make orders for managing the group claims efficiently (lead cases, cut-off dates, etc.). Each claimant remains a separate party and must prove individual losses. A GLO does not merge claims (A), remove the need to prove individual losses (C), or cap damages (D).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant applies for an order requiring the claimant to provide security for costs. The claimant is ordinarily resident outside the UK. What must the defendant show under CPR r.25.13?

options.A: That the claimant is impecunious.
options.B: That the claimant has previously failed to pay costs orders.
options.C: That one of the conditions in CPR r.25.13(2) is met (including that the claimant is ordinarily resident outside the UK) and it is just to make the order in all the circumstances.
options.D: That the claimant's solicitors have no assets within the jurisdiction.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.25.13 allows the court to make an order for security for costs if it is satisfied, having regard to all the circumstances, that it is just to make such an order, and one of the conditions in r.25.13(2) is met. The conditions include: the claimant is ordinarily resident outside the UK (and outside any Brussels/Lugano state); the claimant is a company or other body and there is reason to believe it will be unable to pay the defendant's costs if ordered to do so; or the claimant has changed address since the claim was issued to evade consequences. On these facts, ordinary residence outside the UK is the relevant condition. The defendant does not need to prove impecuniosity separately for this specific condition. Options A, C, and D are either wrong or incomplete.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant is subject to an interim injunction not to contact the claimant. The defendant deliberately contacts the claimant five times in breach of the order. What proceedings may the claimant take?

options.A: The claimant must apply for a fresh injunction.
options.B: The claimant can only seek damages for breach of the injunction.
options.C: The claimant may bring committal proceedings for contempt of court; if proved, the court may impose a fine, sequestration of assets, or imprisonment.
options.D: The breach must be reported to the police — civil courts cannot enforce injunctions.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: An injunction is an order of the court; deliberate breach is contempt of court. The claimant may apply to commit the defendant for contempt under CPR r.81 (committal for breach of a judgment or order). If the contempt is proved to the criminal standard (beyond reasonable doubt), the court may impose a fine, sequestration of assets, or imprisonment. The injunction continues in force; a fresh injunction (A) is not required. Damages (C) are not generally available for breach of a court order in this context — the remedy is contempt. Civil courts have full power to enforce their own orders (D is wrong).

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Two commercial parties have a dispute. Their contract contains an arbitration clause providing for disputes to be resolved by a sole arbitrator. One party issues court proceedings. What can the other party do?

options.A: Nothing — court proceedings override any arbitration clause.
options.B: Seek to strike out the court proceedings for want of jurisdiction.
