options.C: Apply for a stay of the court proceedings under Arbitration Act 1996 s.9; the court must stay the proceedings unless the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative or incapable of being performed.
options.D: Ignore the court proceedings — the arbitrator has exclusive jurisdiction.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Arbitration Act 1996 s.9 provides that a party to an arbitration agreement against whom court proceedings are brought in respect of a matter which under the agreement is to be referred to arbitration may apply to the court to stay those proceedings. The court must grant a stay unless it is satisfied that the arbitration agreement is null and void, inoperative, or incapable of being performed. This is a mandatory stay (subject to the exceptions) which gives effect to the parties' agreement to arbitrate. Option A is wrong. Option B is wrong — the correct mechanism is a s.9 stay, not a strike-out for jurisdiction. Option D is wrong — ignoring court proceedings would result in default judgment.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A construction contractor has a dispute with a developer about the final account on a building project. The contract is a standard JCT form. Which statutory right is available to the contractor?

options.A: To demand immediate arbitration.
options.B: To apply to the court for a summary judgment without issuing proceedings.
options.C: To refer the dispute to adjudication at any time under the Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996 s.108; the adjudicator's decision is binding as an interim measure (enforceable by summary judgment) until the dispute is finally resolved.
options.D: To terminate the contract immediately and claim wasted expenditure.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: The Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996 s.108 gives parties to a qualifying construction contract the right to refer a dispute to adjudication at any time. The adjudicator must reach a decision within 28 days (extendable). The decision is binding until the dispute is finally resolved by arbitration, litigation, or agreement. Courts routinely enforce adjudicator's decisions by summary judgment. This provides a fast-track cash-flow remedy in the construction industry. Option A (immediate arbitration) is a different process with different timescales. Option B is a CPR procedural route, not a statutory right specific to construction. Option D is an extreme remedy not triggered by the mere existence of a dispute.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A solicitor instructed in anticipated litigation commissions a report from an independent surveyor to assist in advising the client on the strength of the claim. Litigation has not yet been issued but is contemplated. The opposing party seeks disclosure. Can litigation privilege be claimed?

options.A: No — litigation privilege only applies once proceedings have been issued.
options.B: No — reports from third parties are never privileged.
options.C: Yes — litigation privilege protects documents created for the dominant purpose of litigation that is reasonably contemplated, even if not yet commenced (Waugh v British Railways Board [1980]).
options.D: Yes — but only if the surveyor is a qualified expert witness.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Litigation privilege protects communications between a lawyer and third party (and between the client and third party) where they were created for the dominant purpose of reasonably contemplated or actual litigation. In Waugh v British Railways Board [1980] the House of Lords confirmed that the dominant purpose test applies. Litigation does not need to be underway — it must be reasonably contemplated. A solicitor's report commissioned to assess the strength of a claim satisfies this test. Option A incorrectly limits litigation privilege to commenced proceedings. Option B is wrong — third party reports can attract litigation privilege if the dominant purpose test is met. Option D introduces an irrelevant qualification requirement.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant suspects the defendant is holding vital evidence which would be destroyed if notice of proceedings was given. The claimant applies for a search order without notice. What is the primary purpose of a search order?

options.A: To allow the claimant's solicitors to enter the defendant's premises to search for and take into custody documents or items relevant to the proceedings.
options.B: To allow the police to conduct a search of the defendant's premises.
options.C: To freeze the defendant's bank accounts.
options.D: To compel the defendant to make witness statements.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: A search order (formerly an Anton Piller order, named after Anton Piller KG v Manufacturing Processes [1976]) is an order that allows the claimant's solicitor (a supervising solicitor) to enter the defendant's premises to search for, inspect, photograph, and take into safekeeping documents or items relevant to the proceedings. It is made without notice to prevent destruction of evidence. It requires: a strong prima facie case; very serious potential or actual damage to the claimant's interests; clear evidence the defendant has relevant documents and there is a real possibility of destruction. Option B is wrong — the police are not involved. Option C describes a freezing injunction. Option D describes an order to give evidence.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A fast-track personal injury claim settles for £18,000 just before trial. Under the Civil Procedure Rules, what costs regime applies to the claimant's costs of the action?

options.A: Costs assessed on the standard basis after a detailed assessment hearing.
options.B: Indemnity costs — because the claim settled before trial.
options.C: Fixed recoverable costs — the claimant recovers fixed amounts prescribed in the CPR costs tables rather than having costs assessed; the amounts are determined by the value and stage of the claim.
options.D: No costs are recoverable on a settled claim.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: From 1 October 2023, fixed recoverable costs (FRC) were extended to almost all fast-track and intermediate-track claims. In fast-track personal injury claims (and many other fast-track claims), the amount of costs recoverable by the winning/recovering party is fixed by reference to the CPR tables, depending on the value of the claim and the stage at which it resolves. This regime applies instead of detailed assessment. At £18,000, FRC will produce a fixed amount. Option A (standard basis detailed assessment) applied to fast-track claims before the 2023 reforms. Option B is wrong. Option D is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A multi-track commercial claim is proceeding in the Business and Property Courts. The solicitors are preparing the disclosure exercise. Which disclosure regime applies?

options.A: Standard disclosure under CPR r.31.6 applies to all multi-track claims.
options.B: Full disclosure of all documents is mandatory in commercial claims.
options.C: The Disclosure Pilot (Practice Direction 57AD) applies to proceedings in the Business and Property Courts; parties must use one of five disclosure models (Models A-E) selected to suit the claim's needs.
options.D: Disclosure only occurs if ordered by the court in a Business and Property Courts claim.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Practice Direction 57AD (the Disclosure Pilot) applies to proceedings in the Business and Property Courts (including the Chancery Division, Commercial Court, etc.). Instead of standard disclosure, parties select from five models: Model A (no standard disclosure), Model B (limited disclosure), Model C (request-based), Model D (standard disclosure equivalent), and Model E (wide search, less common). The pilot aims to make disclosure proportionate and cost-effective for complex commercial litigation. Standard disclosure under r.31.6 (A) does not automatically apply to BPC cases under the pilot. Option B is wrong. Option D is wrong — disclosure is a standing requirement.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Two parties settle litigation shortly before trial. They wish to preserve the enforceability of the settlement terms without making them public. What order should they ask the court to make?

options.A: A consent judgment setting out all settlement terms on the court record.
options.B: A discontinuance notice filed by the claimant.
options.C: A Tomlin order — a consent order staying all further proceedings on terms set out in a schedule, which is not read out in open court; the schedule terms are enforceable by application to lift the stay.
options.D: An unless order incorporating the settlement terms.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: A Tomlin order is a consent order under CPR r.40.6 which: (i) stays all further proceedings on the agreed terms; and (ii) attaches a schedule containing the settlement terms. The schedule is not part of the court record in the same way as the order itself, providing confidentiality. If a party breaches the settlement terms, the other party applies to the court to lift the stay and enforce the schedule terms. A consent judgment (A) would put the settlement terms on the public court record. Discontinuance (C) does not give enforceable status to any settlement terms. An unless order (D) is a case management tool, not a settlement mechanism.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Charging order and order for sale
Dispute Resolution.stem: A judgment creditor holds a judgment for £100,000 against the judgment debtor, who owns a residential property. What enforcement steps can the creditor take in relation to the property?

options.A: The creditor can immediately seize the property.
options.B: The creditor must wait for the debtor to voluntarily sell the property.
options.C: The creditor has no remedy against real property — only personal property can be seized.
options.D: The creditor may apply for a charging order over the property (CPR Part 73); once the charge is registered, the creditor may apply for an order for sale to realise the debt from the property proceeds.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR Part 73 provides for charging orders over land (and securities) to enforce a money judgment. The process: (i) interim charging order made by the court; (ii) final charging order after a hearing; (iii) the charge is registered at the Land Registry; (iv) if the debt remains unpaid, the creditor may then apply for an order for sale of the property under the Charging Orders Act 1979 s.3. The court has a discretion whether to order sale, taking into account the interests of other occupants and secured creditors. Option A is wrong — there is no power of immediate seizure of land in English civil enforcement. Option C is wrong. Option B is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Costs follow the event — CPR r.44.2
Dispute Resolution.stem: The court finds for the claimant on all issues at trial. The court makes no special costs order. What is the default position on costs under CPR r.44.2?

options.A: Each party bears its own costs.
options.B: The court assesses costs immediately at the end of trial.
options.C: Costs are split 50/50 between the parties.
options.D: The unsuccessful party pays the successful party's costs (the general rule under CPR r.44.2(2)(a)), subject to the court's discretion to depart from this where conduct or other factors justify.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.44.2(2)(a) provides that if the court decides to make an order about costs, the general rule is that the unsuccessful party pays the costs of the successful party. The court has a discretion under r.44.2(2)(b) to make a different order. When exercising this discretion, the court considers all the circumstances including conduct (including pre-action conduct), whether a party has succeeded on only part of their case, and any admissible offers to settle. Option A is wrong — each party bearing its own costs is a departure from the general rule. Option C is wrong. Option B is wrong — a detailed assessment may be needed unless a summary assessment is carried out at trial.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Many members of a residents' association have identical claims against a developer for breach of a covenant. They wish to bring a single claim. What procedural option allows one party to represent the others?

options.A: Each member must bring their own individual claim.
options.B: A class action — available only in the Competition Appeal Tribunal.
options.C: A derivative claim under CA 2006 s.260.
options.D: A representative action under CPR r.19.8 — one or more members may represent other members who have the same interest in the claim, provided the court gives permission.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.19.8 provides that where more than one person has the same interest in a claim, the claim may be begun by or against one or more persons who have the same interest as representatives of any other persons who have that interest. This avoids the need for all claimants to be joined individually. Court permission is not strictly required to begin a representative action but the court will oversee the use of the procedure. Derivative claims (C) are for wrongs done to a company under CA 2006 s.260, not covenants disputes. Class actions (D) in the Competition Appeal Tribunal are a separate statutory mechanism. Option A is impractical and not required.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: Diana Pelletier regularly instructs the same forensic accountant on behalf of different clients. The accountant has always provided reports favourable to Pelletier's clients. A colleague suggests Pelletier should always instruct this expert. What professional issue arises?

options.A: No issue — using a reliable expert is good practice.
options.B: Pelletier should always use the same expert to ensure consistency.
options.C: The only concern is whether the expert charges reasonable fees.
options.D: There is a risk that repeated use of an expert who always provides favourable reports may compromise the expert's independence or create a perception of impropriety; Pelletier should not select experts based on expected outcome, and should satisfy herself the expert can give an independent opinion (CPR r.35.3; SRA Code para 1.4).

Dispute Resolution.explanation: An expert's overriding duty is to the court and not to the instructing party (CPR r.35.3). Instructing solicitors must not select experts based on a track record of providing favourable opinions — this can compromise the expert's independence and the integrity of the process. If an expert's reports are consistently one-sided, it may indicate they are not exercising independent judgement. SRA Code para 1.4 requires solicitors not to mislead or attempt to mislead the court or others; fostering arrangements that produce unreliable expert opinions is a form of misleading the court. Option A ignores the independence issue. Option C focuses only on fees. Option B actively encourages the problematic practice.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Duties to client and court — attending counsel
Dispute Resolution.stem: Ravi Sharma is a solicitor attending court with a barrister instructed on his client's behalf. During cross-examination, Sharma learns that a key witness (his client) is about to give evidence he knows to be untrue. The barrister is unaware. What should Sharma do?

options.A: Say nothing — the barrister is responsible for the conduct of the hearing.
options.B: Intervene in open court to correct the client's evidence directly.
options.C: Allow the client to give evidence — it is the court's job to assess credibility.
options.D: Inform the barrister immediately so the barrister can take appropriate steps; ultimately if the client insists on giving false evidence, both Sharma and the barrister must cease acting rather than allow the court to be misled (SRA Code para 2.6; rB4 BSB Handbook).

Dispute Resolution.explanation: SRA Code para 1.4 prohibits making or using false or misleading statements (the duty not to mislead or attempt to mislead the court). The duty not to mislead the court applies to attending solicitors as well as counsel. Sharma must inform the barrister immediately so that appropriate steps (e.g., taking instructions, advising the client, withdrawing) can be taken. If the client refuses to correct the position, both Sharma and the barrister must withdraw rather than permit false evidence to be given. Option A wrongly deflects responsibility. Option C allows the court to be misled. Option B would be procedurally inappropriate and may breach duties of confidence.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A defendant applies for a stay of proceedings to allow the parties to attempt mediation. The claimant objects, arguing that mediation should be voluntary. Following Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil [2023], can the court order a stay for mediation?

options.A: No — courts can only encourage ADR, not compel it.
options.B: Yes — but only with both parties' consent.
options.C: No — mandatory mediation is incompatible with the overriding objective.
options.D: Yes — Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil [2023] confirmed that courts have the power to order or encourage parties to engage in ADR, including granting a stay for mediation, without offending the right of access to court under Article 6 ECHR, provided any order is proportionate.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: In Churchill v Merthyr Tydfil CBC [2023], the Court of Appeal held that courts have the power to order parties to engage in a non-court based dispute resolution process (such as mediation) and to stay proceedings pending that process, without breaching Article 6 ECHR (right of access to court), provided any such order is proportionate and does not shut the party out of the court process permanently. This overturned the previous Halsey view that courts could not compel ADR. The power exists alongside (not instead of) the power to encourage ADR and make adverse costs orders for unreasonable refusal. Option A reflects the pre-Churchill position. Option C is wrong. Option B adds an unnecessary consent requirement.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant sues a defendant for £30,000. The defendant wishes to counterclaim for £45,000 arising from the same transaction. What procedure should the defendant follow?

options.A: Issue separate proceedings for the counterclaim.
options.B: The defendant cannot counterclaim for more than the original claim value.
options.C: Apply to the court for permission before making a counterclaim.
options.D: File a defence and counterclaim in the same document (CPR r.20.4); the counterclaim is treated as a claim and the original claimant becomes the defendant to the counterclaim.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.20.4 allows a defendant to make a counterclaim against the claimant by including it in the defence. The counterclaim may exceed the value of the original claim. No court permission is required if filed with the defence. The parties then stand in the position of both claimant and defendant to each other's claim. Separate proceedings (A) would be wasteful. Permission (C) is not required when the counterclaim is filed with the defence (it is required if filed later without the claimant's consent). Option B is wrong — counterclaims may exceed the original claim value.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Mandatory vs discretionary set-aside — CPR r.13.2
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant enters a default judgment against the defendant, but the defendant had actually filed an acknowledgment of service before the judgment was entered. The claimant's solicitor was unaware. The defendant applies to set aside. Is the set-aside mandatory or discretionary?

options.A: Discretionary — the court must weigh the merits of the defence.
options.B: Mandatory — but the defendant must pay the costs of the failed judgment.
options.C: Discretionary — because the claimant's solicitor acted in good faith.
options.D: Mandatory under CPR r.13.2 — the court must set aside a default judgment if it was wrongly entered (e.g., the defendant had already filed an acknowledgment of service).

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.13.2 provides that the court must set aside a judgment entered in default if the defendant had filed an acknowledgment of service (or a defence) before judgment was entered, or if the whole of the claim was satisfied before judgment was entered, or if the defendant filed a certificate of satisfaction. In these circumstances, the judgment was irregularly entered and set-aside is mandatory — the court has no discretion. This differs from CPR r.13.3 (discretionary set-aside where the judgment was regularly entered). Option A and C introduce discretion where the rule is mandatory. Option B is incorrect — costs consequences depend on the circumstances but mandatory set-aside does not automatically impose costs on the defendant.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: After a defence is filed in a defended case, the court sends both parties a directions questionnaire. What is the purpose of this document?

options.A: To set out the parties' final evidence before trial.
options.B: To require the parties to estimate the total value of the claim.
options.C: To formally request that the other party engage in mediation.
options.D: To provide information to assist the court in allocating the case to a track and giving appropriate directions for case management (CPR r.26).

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Under CPR r.26.3, once a defence is filed, the court will serve a directions questionnaire (DQ) on each party. The DQ requests information including: proposed track, costs estimate, whether the party has complied with any pre-action protocol, whether the parties have considered ADR, whether a stay for ADR is sought, proposed timetable, expert evidence, and trial time estimate. The court uses the completed DQs to allocate the case to a track and issue case management directions. Option A is wrong — evidence is exchanged later (witness statements, expert reports). Option C is part of the DQ but not its sole purpose. Option B is not the function of the DQ.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Conditional fee agreements — success fee recoverability
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant's solicitor acts under a conditional fee agreement with a 25% success fee. The claimant wins. Can the success fee be recovered from the losing defendant?

options.A: Yes — success fees are always recoverable from the losing party.
options.B: No — CFAs are unlawful and cannot be used by solicitors.
options.C: Yes — success fees can be recovered if the CFA was approved by the court.
options.D: No — under the Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 (LASPO), success fees under CFAs entered into on or after 1 April 2013 are not recoverable from the losing party; they must be met from the client's damages.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: LASPO 2012 abolished the recoverability of CFA success fees and ATE insurance premiums from losing parties for cases where the CFA was entered into on or after 1 April 2013 (with some exceptions including publication and privacy cases). In most cases, the success fee comes out of the client's damages (subject to the 25% cap on damages in personal injury cases). CFAs are entirely lawful (D is wrong). Option A reflects the pre-LASPO position. Option C invents a court approval process that does not exist.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Without prejudice save as to costs correspondence
Dispute Resolution.stem: Gerald Crane, acting for the claimant, writes a letter to the defendant's solicitors marked 'Without Prejudice Save as to Costs', offering to settle for £70,000. The defendant rejects the offer, and at trial the claimant is awarded £75,000. At the costs hearing, can the letter be shown to the judge?

options.A: No — all without prejudice correspondence is protected from disclosure at any stage.
options.B: No — the letter has the same effect as a Part 36 offer and the court will not look at it.
options.C: Yes — but only if both parties consent.
options.D: Yes — 'Without Prejudice Save as to Costs' correspondence is protected from the trial judge but can be referred to at the costs hearing, enabling the court to consider the defendant's rejection of the offer when deciding costs.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: The label 'Without Prejudice Save as to Costs' creates a hybrid form of protection: the letter is privileged during the main trial (so the trial judge does not see it when determining liability and quantum), but it may be produced at the costs hearing to allow the court to consider whether the rejecting party acted reasonably. This is distinct from a without prejudice letter (which is protected at all stages) and from a Part 36 offer (which triggers specific cost consequences). The defendant's rejection of a reasonable settlement offer can result in adverse cost consequences under CPR r.44.2. Option A overstates the 'without prejudice' protection. Option C is wrong — consent of both parties is not required for the costs hearing use. Option B is incorrect.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party wishes to rely on a witness statement made by a witness who has since died. The opposing party objects that it is hearsay. Under the Civil Evidence Act 1995, is this admissible?

options.A: No — hearsay is inadmissible in civil proceedings.
options.B: No — statements of deceased witnesses must be authenticated by a notary.
options.C: Yes — but only if the statement was made under oath.
options.D: Yes — under Civil Evidence Act 1995 s.1, hearsay evidence is admissible in civil proceedings subject to the procedural requirements; the court will consider its weight and reliability.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Civil Evidence Act 1995 s.1 abolished the rule against hearsay in civil proceedings. Hearsay evidence is admissible in civil cases. Section 2 requires a party relying on hearsay to give notice, and the court will assess the weight to give to the evidence having regard to factors such as whether the maker could have been called and whether the evidence is consistent with other evidence. A witness having died is a reason why the person cannot be called and is relevant to the weight assessment. Option A states the old common law position, now abolished. Option C introduces an oath requirement which does not exist. Option B is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.stem: A claimant issues proceedings against the main contractor for defective building works. It later becomes clear that the sub-contractor is also responsible. Under CPR r.19.2, what can the claimant do?

options.A: Issue separate proceedings against the sub-contractor — parties cannot be added after issue.
options.B: Apply for a joinder of causes of action.
options.C: Apply for a group litigation order.
options.D: Apply to the court to add the sub-contractor as a party (CPR r.19.2); the court will grant permission if it is desirable to add the party so that the court can resolve all the matters in dispute.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: CPR r.19.2 allows the court to order a person to be added as a new party to proceedings if it is desirable to add the new party so that the court can resolve all the matters in dispute in the proceedings. The court has discretion and considers whether justice requires the addition and whether it is efficient to do so. Adding the sub-contractor avoids separate proceedings and allows all issues to be resolved together. Option A is wrong — parties can be added after issue with the court's permission. Option C is for multiple claims with common issues, not joinder. Option B is not a recognised CPR mechanism.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Pro bono costs orders — s.194 Legal Services Act 2007
Dispute Resolution.stem: A party is represented pro bono (free of charge) by a solicitor and wins at trial. Can a costs order be made in that party's favour?

options.A: No — costs orders can only be made in favour of a party who has incurred costs.
options.B: No — pro bono representation creates no entitlement to costs in any circumstances.
options.C: Yes — but the winning party receives the costs personally.
options.D: Yes — under s.194 Legal Services Act 2007, the court may make a costs order in favour of a party who received free legal representation; the costs are paid to a prescribed charity (the Access to Justice Foundation).

Dispute Resolution.explanation: Legal Services Act 2007 s.194 provides that where a party to proceedings has received pro bono representation and a costs order is made in that party's favour, the court may order the other party to pay an amount to the prescribed charity (the Access to Justice Foundation). This mechanism: (i) ensures that pro bono work does not give the opponent a financial advantage; and (ii) generates funds for access to justice. Option A is the old common law rule which s.194 modifies. Option C is wrong — the payment goes to the charity, not the party. Option B is wrong.

Dispute Resolution.subject: Dispute Resolution
Dispute Resolution.subtopic: Duty to advise on litigation risk and costs
Dispute Resolution.stem: Ravi Sharma is advising Hassan Nazari on a breach of contract claim worth £25,000. Sharma estimates litigation costs of £35,000 to trial. Sharma does not advise Nazari of the costs risk. Which SRA Code obligation is engaged?

options.A: Duty to maintain client confidentiality.
options.B: Duty to report the case to the Legal Ombudsman.
options.C: Duty not to mislead the court.
options.D: Duty to act in Nazari's best interests (SRA Code para 1.2) by giving him the information he needs to make an informed decision, including advising on the disproportionate cost-benefit position — where litigation costs substantially exceed the potential recovery, the client must be advised so he can make an informed decision whether to proceed.

Dispute Resolution.explanation: SRA Principle 7 requires solicitors to act in the best interests of each client. Para 8.6 requires giving clients the best information they reasonably can about costs. Where the anticipated litigation costs substantially exceed the potential damages (£35,000 in costs vs £25,000 claim), the solicitor must advise the client clearly so the client can make an informed decision. Failure to do so deprives the client of a material consideration in deciding whether to litigate, settle, or pursue ADR. The other options are not engaged by a failure to give costs advice.
